verdict_reasoning: ABCB9 (TAPL) is a multi-pass TM ABC transporter that resides predominantly in lysosomal/late-endosomal membranes, where it translocates peptides from the cytosol into the lysosomal lumen. It is not a plasma-membrane-resident transporter at baseline. However, lysosomal membrane proteins can reach the plasma membrane via lysosomal exocytosis, a well-documented process in which lysosomes fuse with the plasma membrane and transiently expose their limiting-membrane proteins on the cell surface. ABCB9 has a multi-pass TM architecture and resides in the lysosomal limiting membrane; lysosomal exocytosis therefore provides a plausible and class-supported route to outer-leaflet accessibility. Pre-no checklist: no dominant cancer/cell-death ecto-pool reported; not germline-restricted; no activation-induced PM pool documented; no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering; lysosomal TM protein → lysosomal_exocytosis applies.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Gene-specific experimental evidence for ABCB9 surface display via lysosomal exocytosis is lacking; the call rests on class-level lysosomal-TM-protein biology rather than protein-specific data.
